items.correct: Subtle hints or clues earlier in a text that anticipate later events, making the resolution feel both surprising and inevitable.
items.misconception: Foreshadowing is when a character predicts the future.
items.random_wrong: Foreshadowing is the shadow a character casts in a scene.
items.why: Foreshadowing works on the reader, not within the story world. A character's prediction is a plot event — foreshadowing is an authorial technique embedded in detail and tone.

items.q: What is the function of dystopian fiction?
items.correct: To critique tendencies in the writer's own society by extrapolating them into a nightmarish future — dystopias hold up a warning mirror to the present.
items.misconception: Dystopian fiction is just storytelling set in scary futures.
items.random_wrong: Dystopian fiction predicts what will actually happen in 100 years.
items.why: Dystopias are about the present, not the future. 1984 critiques 1948; The Handmaid's Tale critiques 1980s America. Students miss the satirical function and read them as prophecy.

items.correct: The claim that judging a text by what the author 'intended' is fallacious — the text's meaning lies in the work itself, not the author's stated or guessed intentions.
items.misconception: It means authors can never make mistakes when writing.
items.random_wrong: It means it is wrong to write fiction.
items.why: Students assume 'author intent' settles meaning. Wimsatt and Beardsley argued it doesn't — meaning is a public property of the text, not a private fact about the author.

items.q: Which dramatic tradition does the play echo?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Morality play. “Restoration comedy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: With a telephone call about a new death
items.why: It's With a telephone call about a new death. “With a wedding” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the play first performed?
items.why: The real figure is 1945. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
